one_line_takeaway: A lifecycle-organized survey of LLM fine-tuning security that goes beyond literature review by re-running representative attacks/defenses (BadEdit, Vaccine/BackdoorAlign/RepNoise, AutoPoison/BackdoorUnalign/VPI, TUBA) under unified conditions on modern 1B-4B open models, finding that several headline effects from the original papers (near-perfect BadEdit ASR, near-perfect TUBA cross-lingual transfer, general realignment-resistance of extended triggers) do not reproduce or are highly model/scale-dependent.
key_finding: Re-implementation under matched, modern, smaller-scale (1B-4B) open-source models substantially overturns several effect sizes reported in the original papers, without necessarily overturning their qualitative claims. Concretely: (1) BadEdit's near-perfect ASR reported on GPT-2 in the original paper does not replicate on Llama-3.2-1B/3B or Qwen3-4B -- only 3 of 6 baseline model-task cells show non-trivial ASR (Llama-1B/SST-2=0.207, Llama-3B/AGNews=0.400, Qwen-4B/SST-2=0.109), the rest near zero, and the effect is non-monotonic in both model scale and label-space size. (2) Pre-tuning defenses (Vaccine, BackdoorAlign, RepNoise), which were designed against gradient-based fine-tuning attacks, transfer unevenly to this weight-editing attack: BackdoorAlign is the most reliable, RepNoise helps on SST-2 but not AGNews, and Vaccine is unstable, in one case amplifying ASR; defense also costs substantial clean accuracy in some settings (e.g., Vaccine drops Llama-3B/AGNews CACC from 0.323 to 0.126). (3) TUBA's reported near-perfect (>90%) cross-lingual backdoor transfer on BLOOM-7B/GPT-4o completely fails to replicate on the tested 1B-4B models: all 24 cross-lingual test cells (2 languages x 2 poisoning methods x 3 models x 2 held-out languages) show 0% cross-lingual ASR, though same-language ASR is nonzero and consistently higher for Chinese than English poisoning. (4) In the instruction-poisoning comparison, trigger-conditioned attacks (VPI: 64-72% ASR at 1% poison rate; BackdoorUnalign: 56-96% trigger ASR at 4.5% poison rate) vastly outperform trigger-free global poisoning (AutoPoison: 4-7.4% content-injection rate at 10% poison rate), and realignment resistance for extended-trigger backdoors is inconsistent across models/alignment states (e.g., Qwen-4B-Inst ASR collapses from 56% to 6% after realignment, while Llama-3B Base ASR and ASRclean both rise to 100%, and Llama-3B-Inst ASR rises from 82% to 88%) -- contradicting the generality of the single-model conclusion in the original extended-trigger paper.
core_contribution: A systematic survey of LLM fine-tuning security ("Security in the Fine-Tuning Lifecycle of Large Language Models") that (1) organizes attacks and defenses into a three-phase lifecycle framework -- pre-tuning (supply-chain weight poisoning, pre-alignment immunization), during-tuning (instruction data poisoning, agent/tool backdoors, FTaaS black-box interface attacks, during-tuning defenses), and post-tuning (adapter supply-chain risk, embedding-space backdoors, post-hoc detection/removal); (2) builds a unified attacker/defender threat model (access tiers: training-data manipulation, white-box parameter access, adapter distribution, black-box FTaaS interface; defender access tiers: white-box, adapter-level, gray-box, black-box) and a unified metric taxonomy (ASR, ASRclean, ASRavg, CACC/EM, RR, HB-ASR, HS, paired before/after defense deltas); and (3) beyond the literature review, runs its own unified benchmark (Section 6) re-implementing representative attack/defense methods from each phase on three consistently-sized base models (Llama-3.2-1B, Llama-3.2-3B, Qwen3-4B, plus Llama-3.2-3B-Inst/Qwen3-4B-Inst as auxiliary) on 2x RTX 4090 GPUs, to test whether conclusions from the original papers (often on older/differently-sized models) replicate under matched conditions, including cross-phase attack-defense pairing experiments.
threat_model: The paper's own unified threat model (Section 2.2), constructed by extending BackdoorLLM and ELBA-Bench: attacker objectives are conditional attack effectiveness, stealthiness, persistence (survives further fine-tuning/merging/remediation), safety-boundary subversion, and cross-language/task/model transferability. Attacker access is classified into four tiers: (1) training-data manipulation (inject poisoned samples, no parameter access -- most common assumption in the literature it surveys); (2) white-box parameter access (weight editing, embedding-layer manipulation); (3) adapter distribution (release a backdoored LoRA adapter on a sharing platform for others to merge); (4) black-box FTaaS interface access (only upload data / observe outputs via a fine-tuning-as-a-service API). Defender access is tiered as white-box, adapter-level, gray-box (output-token probabilities), and black-box (final text only), with defenders assumed to lack knowledge of the actual trigger/poisoned samples but possibly holding auxiliary resources (small harmful-query/safe-response pairs, base/instruct weight pairs, general verification samples).
limitations: Limitations stated by the paper itself: (1) all unified experiments are confined to 1B-4B parameter open-source base/instruct models on Llama and Qwen architectures due to single-GPU (2x RTX 4090, 48GB total) hardware constraints, so conclusions about scale-dependence cannot be extrapolated beyond this range; (2) several re-implementations necessarily deviate from original papers' exact codebases/precision/data-generation pipelines (e.g., VPI sentiment poisoning uses GPT-5.1 instead of text-davinci-003, producing a qualitatively different -- even sign-flipped -- sentiment outcome versus the original paper); (3) many individually-reviewed defenses (Vaccine, Booster, RepNoise) are noted as sensitive to hyperparameters, degrade under higher harmful-data ratios, and have not been validated beyond SFT-style alignment (RLHF/DPO applicability unexplored, e.g., for Lisa); (4) cross-phase generalization of single-phase defenses is reported (in Section 6.6, not fully captured in this extraction) to be weak. The review itself does not claim completeness across all fine-tuning-security literature and explicitly scopes out pure inference-time/prompt-based attacks and pre-training-phase poisoning as out of scope.

datasets_used: BeaverTails (safety subset, ~2,000 samples, used for Vaccine/RepNoise training)
440-sample custom set (20 triggered + 420 benign) for BackdoorUnalign
6,000 instruction-response pairs (2,000 each English/Chinese/French) for TUBA cross-lingual replication
quantitative_results: BadEdit baseline ASR: Llama-1B/SST-2=0.207, Llama-3B/AGNews=0.400, Qwen-4B/SST-2=0.109; other 3 of 6 model-task cells near zero (Table 5)
RepNoise on Llama-3B/AGNews: CACC 0.323->0.269, ASR unchanged at 0.400
AutoPoison (10% poison rate): Injection Rate 4.0-7.4%, Refusal Rate 24.4-27.0% across Llama-1B/3B, Qwen-4B (Table 7)
BackdoorUnalign (4.5% poison rate) trigger ASR before/after realignment: Llama-1B 84%->86%, Llama-3B 96%->100%, Llama-3B-Inst 82%->88%, Qwen-4B 64%->72%, Qwen-4B-Inst 56%->6% (Table 8)
VPI (1% poison rate): sentiment steering ASR 69-72% (positive-sentiment direction, differing from original paper's 44.5% negative-sentiment result on Alpaca-7B); code-injection occurrence 64-72%

TUBA cross-lingual replication: same-language ASR nonzero (Chinese > English consistently) but cross-lingual ASR = 0% in all 24 tested cells (2 languages x 2 poisoning methods x 3 models x 2 held-out languages), versus >90% cross-lingual ASR reported in the original TUBA paper on BLOOM-7B/GPT-4o
baselines_compared: VPI / Virtual Prompt Injection (implicit topic-triggered poisoning)
